§ 9. No suit shall be maintained in any court to
restrain or delay the collection or payment of the
tax herein imposed for any reason whatever , but
the aggrieved taxpayer shall pay the tax as and
when due , and if paid under protest may at any
time within two years from the date of such pay-
ment sue the Auditor of Public Accounts in an ac-
tion at law to recover the tax so paid , with legal in-
terest thereon from the date of its payment . If it is
legally determined that said tax , or any part
thereof, was wrongfully collected for any reason ,
it shall be the duty of the Auditor of Public Ac-
counts then in office to issue his warrant on the
Treasurer of the Commonwealth of Kentucky for
the amount of such tax so adjudged to have been
wrongfully collected , together with interest thereon ,
and the Treasurer shall pay same out of the public
road fund of the State . A separate suit need not be
filed for each separate payment made by any tax-
payer , and a recovery may be had in one suit for as
many illegal payments as may have been made , and
which are not barred by the limitation of two years
herein imposed .

of office has expired to make his settlement with the

ately thereafter he shall deliver these records to his
successor . On the failure of any outgoing sheriff

dollars ( $500.00 ) and be liable on his bond for any
default .

clerk of the board of supervisors , and shall keep an
accurate journal or record of the proceedings and
orders of the board and shall correct the tax books
in accordance with the action of the board , and for

same compensation per day as the members of the
board and be paid in the same way, provided how-
ever , that he shall be allowed not exceeding five days
after adjournment of the board for correction of the
tax books .

duplicate on written or printed forms , and deliver

erty has been raised or assessed by the board , show-
ing the amount of raise or assessment , and the date
when the board will reconvene for the purpose of

AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation , and defining what
constitutes "residence " for purposes of taxation of intangible

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That any person who has had his actual or
habitual place of abode in this State for the larger
portion of the twelve months next preceding the
date as of which an assessment is due to be made ,
shall be deemed a resident of this State for the pur-
pose of taxation , and any intangible property which
he may own shall be taxable therein , unless on or
before that date he has changed his place of abode
to a place without this State with the bona fide in-
tention of continuing actually to abide permanently
without this State . The fact that a person who has
so changed his actual place of abode , within six
months from so doing, again abides within this
State , shall be prima facie evidence that he did not
intend permanently to have his actual place of
abode without this State . Such person , so chang-
ing his actual place of abode and not intending per-
manently to continue it without this State and not
having listed his property for taxation as a resident
of this State , for the purpose of having his prop-

CHAPTER 134.
AN ACT amending and re -enacting Section 4072 , of Kentucky
Statutes , Carroll's edition 1922 , relating to advancement of
money to defray expenses and partial payment on salaries of
the county tax commissioner in counties containing a city of
the first class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. In counties containing cities of the first
class , the county tax commissioner shall be entitled
to receive an advancement of five thousand ($5 ,-
000.00 ) dollars per month to defray necessary offi-
cial expenses and partial payment upon the salaries
of himself and his deputies and assistants , and the
Auditor of Public Accounts shall on the first of each
calendar month draw his warrant on the treasurer
in favor of such county tax commissioner for such
sum . The sum of such advancements shall be de-
ducted from the total amount found to be due such
tax commissioner when yearly settlements are
made . Should the tax commissioner in such county
die , resign or be removed from office , or should the
office of county tax commissioner in such county for
any cause become vacant , the sums advanced here-
under shall be deducted from the total sum due for
making assessment .
All laws and parts of laws in conflict herewith are
hereby repealed .
Approved March 21 , 1928 .

§ 1. Every public official of any county , graded

clude in or attach to said report as a part thereof a

such officer shall within thirty days after the close
of the fiscal year cause such statement to be pub-
lished in full in a newspaper published in said county
which has the largest circulation therein , and said
officer shall file a written or printed copy of said
statement , subscribed and sworn to , in the office of
the clerk of the county court of the county in which
said officer resides , or holds office . Provided , that
the publication required in this act shall be made in
the size of type provided by law and at the rate

ment .

CHAPTER 138 .
AN ACT to amend and re -enact Sections 1 and 2 of Chapter
166 of the Acts of the General Assembly of 1926 , approved
March 19 , 1926 , entitled "An Act relating to revenue and taxa-
tion ; providing license or excise tax on the business or
occupation of manufacturing distilled spirits , known as whisky
or brandy , or other species of such double stamp spirits in
this State , and /or the business or occupation of a warehouse-
man storing such distilled spirits ; " etc.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That section 1 of chapter 166 of the Acts of
the General Assembly of 1926 , approved March 19 ,
1926 , and relating to license taxes on the business or
occupation of a warehouseman storing distilled
spirits , be, and the same is hereby amended and re-
enacted , so that when amended and re -enacted said
section 1 shall read as follows :
" Section 1. Every owner or proprietor of a
bonded warehouse in this State in which distilled
spirits , known as whiskey or brandy , or other species
of such double stamp spirits , are now or hereafter
stored , shall, in addition to the taxes now imposed
by law, pay to the Commonwealth of Kentucky a
license tax at the rate herein set out on every proof
gallon of said distilled spirits which is liable for tax
to the Federal Government as shown by its official
records , and the amount of the tax due to the State
at the rates herein set out shall be thereby fixed and
measured by the State Tax Commission . The license
tax upon such distilled spirits as were manufactured
or produced prior to the passage of this Act shall
be ten cents ( 10c ) per proof gallon determined as
aforesaid , and the license tax upon such distilled
spirits as shall be manufactured or produced after
the passage of this Act shall be two cents ( 2c ) per
proof gallon determined as aforesaid . "

CHAPTER 139 .
AN ACT to repeal and re -enact subsection 3 of Section 3704 of
Carroll's Kentucky Statutes , 1922 edition , relating to the power
of the board of trustees of sixth class towns to levy and col-
lect property tax .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That subsection 3 of section 3704 of Car-
roll's Kentucky Statutes , 1922 edition, be repealed
and re-enacted so that the same , when thus re -en-
acted , shall read as follows :
Levy and collect property tax. To levy and col-
lect annually a property tax , not exceeding Seventy-
Five Cents ( 75c ) on the One Hundred Dollars
($100.00 ) valuation .
§ 2. All acts and parts of acts in conflict here-
with are hereby repealed .
Approved March 17 , 1928 .
CHAPTER 140.
AN ACT concerning the duties of the county clerk in counties
containing cities of the first class in making out tax bills and
certifying same to sheriff for collection .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That in addition to the duties now imposed
upon the clerks of each county court by sections four
thousand two hundred thirty -nine -a to four thousand
two hundred thirty-nine -one, both inclusive , Ken-
tucky Statutes , the county clerks of each county con-
taining a city of the first class , shall at the time of
making out the said tax bills for certification to
sheriff for collection , cause to be inserted in front of
every name appearing on the assessment books of
the County Tax Commissioner of such county , the
bill number of the tax bill certified to the sheriff .

sales of real estate belonging to said institutions .

sembly attempted to confer upon the Boards of Regents of the
Western State Normal School and Teachers College , and the
Eastern State Normal School and Teachers College , which at
that date were the Western State Normal School , and the
Eastern State Normal School , the power and authority to sell
and convey any real estate or building owned by said institu-
tions when said boards should regard it for the best interest
of the institution so to do and to reinvest the proceeds in
order ( other ) real estate and buildings for the use of said
schools , and whereas it appears that the act in so far as it
conferred such authority upon said Board of Regents was and
is void as violative of Section 51 of the Constitution of Ken-
tucky , and whereas said Boards of Regents have acted pur-
suant to the provisions of said act and have sold and con-
veyed real estate and buildings between the date of the enact-
ment of said act and the date of the enactment of chapter 60
of the Acts of 1924 which legally conferred authority on said
boards to sell and convey such real estate and whereas there
is some doubt as to the validity of the titles to the property

of this act shall be fined not less than twenty - five
dollars nor more than fifty dollars for each offense .
The clerk or deputy who actually receives and files

All fines collected under this section shall go in
the common school fund for this state .
Any person or persons who shall , willfully and

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :

CHAPTER 148 .
AN ACT relating to the law of sales of goods .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
PART I.
Formation of the Contract .
§ 1. ( Contracts to Sell and Sales . ) ( 1. ) A con-
tract to sell goods is a contract whereby the seller
agrees to transfer the property in goods to the
buyer for a consideration called the price .
( 2) A sale of goods is an agreement whereby the
seller transfers the property in goods to the buyer
for a consideration called the price .
( 3) A contract to sell or a sale may be absolute
or conditional .
(4) There may be a contract to sell or a sale be-
tween one part owner and another .
§ 2. ( Capacity-Liabilities for Necessaries . )
Capacity to buy and sell is regulated by the general
law concerning capacity to contract , and to transfer
and acquire property .

The Price .
§ 9. (Definition and Ascertainment of Price . )
(1) The price may be fixed by the contract , or may
be left to be fixed in such manner as may be agreed ,
or it may be determined by the course of dealing

( 2) The price may be made payable in any per-
sonal property .

part of the consideration for transferring or for
promising to transfer the property in goods , this
act shall not apply .
(4 ) Where the price is not determined in accord-
ance with the foregoing provisions the buyer must
pay a reasonable price . What is a reasonable price
is a question of fact dependent on the circumstances
of each particular case .
§ 10. ( Sale at a Valuation . ) ( 1 ) Where there is
a contract to sell or a sale of goods at a price or on
terms to be fixed by a third person , and such third
person without fault of the seller or the buyer, can-
not or does not fix the price or terms , the contract
or the sale is thereby avoided ; but if the goods or
any part thereof have been delivered to and ap-
propriated by the buyer he must pay a reasonable
price therefor .
(2 ) Where such third person is prevented from
fixing the price or terms by fault of the seller or the
buyer, the party not in fault may have such remedies
against the party in fault as are allowed by Parts
IV and V of this act .

affirmation of fact or any promise by the seller re-
lating to the goods is an express warranty if the
natural tendency of such affirmation or promise is to
induce the buyer to purchase the goods , and if the
buyer purchases the goods relying thereon . No

opinion only shall be construed as a warranty .

PART III .
Performance of the Contract .
§ 41. ( Seller Must Deliver and Buyer Accept
Goods . ) It is the duty of the seller to deliver the
goods , and of the buyer to accept and pay for them ,
in accordance with the terms of the contract to sell
or sale .
§ 42. (Delivery and Payment are Concurrent
Conditions . ) Unless otherwise agreed , delivery of
the goods and payment of the price are concurrent
conditions ; that is to say , the seller must be ready
and willing to give possession of the goods to the
buyer in exchange for the price and the buyer must
be ready and willing to pay the price in exchange
for possession of the goods .
$ 43 . ( Place , Time and Manner of Delivery . )
(1 ) Whether it is for the buyer to take possession
of the goods or for the seller to send them to the
buyer is a question depending in each case on the
contract , express or implied , between the parties .

§ 46. (Delivery to a Carrier on Behalf of the
Buyer .) ( 1 ) Where , in pursuance of a contract to

a carrier , whether named by the buyer or not , for

and the goods are lost or damaged in course of tran-
sit , the buyer may decline to treat the delivery to
the carrier as a delivery to himself , or may hold the
seller responsible in damages .
( 3) Unless otherwise agreed , where goods are
sent by the seller to the buyer under circumstances
in which the seller knows or ought to know that it
is usual to insure , the seller must give such notice to
the buyer as may enable him to insure them during
their transit , and if the seller fails to do so , the
goods shall be deemed to be at his risk during such
transit .
§ 47. (Right to Examine the Goods . ) ( 1 ) Where

cepted them unless and until he has had a reason-
able opportunity of examining them for the purpose

tenders delivery of goods to the buyer , he is bound ,
on request , to afford the buyer a reasonable oppor-
tunity of examining the goods for the purpose of

classification.summary: This statute outlines the responsibilities and liabilities of a buyer who fails to accept delivery of goods when the seller is ready and willing to deliver. It defines an unpaid seller and the remedies available to them.

Therefore, it is classified as not being a Jim Crow law.

UNPAID SELLER'S LIEN
$ 54. (When Right of Lien May be Exercised . )
(1 ) Subject to the provisions of this act , the un-
paid seller of goods who is in possession of them is
entitled to retain possession of them until payment
or tender of the price in the following cases , namely :
( a ) Where the goods have been sold without any
stipulation as to credit ;
(b) Where the goods have been sold on credit ,
but the term of credit has expired ;
(c ) Where the buyer becomes insolvent .
(2 ) The seller may exercise his right of lien not-
withstanding that he is in possession of the goods
as agent or bailee for the buyer .
§ 55. (Lien After Part Delivery . ) Where an
unpaid seller has made part delivery of the goods ,
he may exercise his right of lien on the remainder ,
unless such part delivery has been made under such
circumstances as to show an intent to waive the lien
or right of retention .

506 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
•§ 56. (When Lien is Lost . ) ( 1 ) The unpaid
seller of goods loses his lien thereon-
(a) When he delivers the goods to a carrier or
other bailee for the purpose of transmission to the
buyer without reserving the property in the goods
or the right to the possession thereof ;
(b ) When the buyer or his agent lawfully ob-
tains possession of the goods ;
(c) By waiver thereof.
( 2 ) The unpaid seller of goods , having a lien
thereon , does no lose his lien by reason only that.
he has obtained judgment or decree for the price of
the goods .
STOPPAGE IN TRANSITU .
§ 57. ( Seller May Stop Goods on Buyer's In-
solvency . ) Subject to the provisions of this act ,
when the buyer of goods is or becomes insolvent ,
the unpaid seller who has parted with the possession
of the goods has the right of stopping them in
transitu , that is to say, he may resume possession of
the goods at any time while they are in transit , and
he will then become entitled to the same rights in
regard to the goods as he would have had if he had
never parted with the possession .
$ 58 . (When Goods are in Transit . ) ( 1 ) Goods
are in transit within the meaning of section 57—
( a ) From the time when they are delivered to a
carrier by land or water , or other bailee for the pur-
pose of transmission to the buyer , until the buyer ,
or his agent in that behalf, takes delivery of them
from such carrier or other bailee ;
(b ) If the goods are rejected by the buyer , and
the carrier or other bailee continues in possession
of them , even if the seller has refused to receive
them back .
(2 ) Goods are no longer in transit within the
meaning of section 57 :

§ 59. (Ways of Exercising the Right to Stop . )
( 1 ) The unpaid seller may exercise his right of
stoppage in transitu either by obtaining actual pos-
session of the goods or by giving notice of his claim

any issue involving the question whether the buyer
had been in default an unreasonable time before the

buyer's repudiation or countermand . The profit the
seller would have made if the contract or the sale
had been fully performed shall be considered in

§ 69. ( Remedies for Breach of Warranty .)
Where there is a breach of warranty by the seller ,
the buyer may, at his election-
(a) Accept or keep the goods and set up against
the seller , the breach of warranty by way of recoup-
ment in diminution or extinction of the price ;
(b) Accept or keep the goods and maintain an
action against the seller for damages for the breach
of warranty ;
(c) Refuse to accept the goods , if the property
therein has not passed , and maintain an action
against the seller for damages for the breach of
warranty ;
(d ) Rescind the contract to sell or the sale and
refuse to receive the goods , or if the goods have
already been received , return them or offer to re-
turn them to the seller and recover the price or any
part thereof which has been paid .
(2 ) When the buyer has claimed and been
granted a remedy in any one of these ways , no other
remedy can thereafter be granted .
(3) Where the goods have been delivered to the
buyer, he cannot rescind the sale if he knew of the
breach of warranty when he accepted the goods , or
if he fails to notify the seller within a reasonable
time of the election to rescind , or if he fails to re-
turn or to offer to return the goods to the seller in
substantially as good condition as they were in at
the time the property was transferred to the buyer .
But if deterioration or injury of the goods is due to

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 515
PART VI.
INTERPRETATION .
§ 71. ( Variation of Implied Obligations . ) Where
any right , duty or liability would arise under a con-
tract to sell or a sale by implication of law , it may
be negatived or varied by express agreement or by
course of dealing between the parties , or by custom ,
if the custom be such as to bind both parties to the
contract of the sale .
§ 72. ( Rights May be Enforced by Action . )
Where any right , duty or liability is declared by this
act , it may, unless otherwise by this act provided ,
be enforced by action .
§ 73. ( Rule for Cases Not Provided for by This
Act. ) In any case not provided for in this act , the
rules of law and equity, including the law merchant ,
and in particular the rules relating to the law of
principal and agent and to the effect of fraud , mis-
representation , duress or coercion , mistake , bank-
ruptcy , or other invalidating cause , shall continue to
apply to contracts to sell and to sales of goods .
§ 74. (Interpretation Shall Give Effect to Pur-
pose of Uniformity . ) This act shall be so inter-
preted and construed , as to effectuate its general
purpose to make uniform the laws of those states
which enact it.
§ 75. (Provisions Not Applicable to Mortgages . )
The provisions of this act relating to contracts to
sell and to sales do not apply , unless so stated , to
any transaction in the form of a contract to sell or
a sale which is intended to operate by way of mort-
gage , pledge , charge , or other security.
§ 76. (Definitions . ) ( 1 ) In this act , unless the
context or subject matter otherwise requires-
"Action " includes counterclaim, set-off and suit
in equity.

" Buyer " means a person who buys or agrees to

"Fault" means wrongful act or default .
" Fungible goods " means goods of which any unit

as the equivalent of any other unit .
"Future goods " means good to be manufac-
tured or acquired by the seller after making of the

" Goods " include all chattels personal other than

plements , industrial growing crops , and things at-

"Order " in sections of this act relating to docu-

CHAPTER 152 .
AN ACT giving the father and mother equal rights to the serv-
ices and earnings of their minor children , and to maintain
actions for loss of the same .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That the father and mother of a minor child are
equally entitled to its services and earnings . If one
of the parents are dead , or has abandoned the child ,
or been deprived of its custody by court decree , the
other is entitled to such services and earnings . The
parents jointly may maintain an action for loss of
the wages or services of their minor child when such
loss is occasioned by an injury wrongfully or negli-
gently inflicted upon such child . But if either the
father or mother be dead , or has abandoned the
child , or been deprived of its custody by court de-
cree , or refuses to sue, the other may sue alone ; pro-
vided , nothing contained herein shall be deemed to
supersede , limit , modify or affect the existing law
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1928, p. 578

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That if any person shall wilfully or knowingly
mine or remove coal from the lands or premises of
another of the value of twenty ( $ 20.00 ) dollars or
more , without color of title in himself to the coal
so mined and removed , he shall be guilty of a mis-
demeanor and shall be fined not less than one hun-
dred ( $ 100.00 ) dollars nor more than five thousand
($ 5,000.00 ) dollars , and in addition be liable in dam-
ages to the owner , double the market value of such
coal so wrongfully mined and removed . The word
"person" as herein shall include firms , associa-
tions , partnerships and corporations .

AN ACT to require all owners and lessees of all ferries op-
erated on any stream in this Commonwealth , where roads and

primary road system by being actually surveyed and contracts
let for the building of same , and State maintained highways ,
cross , to grade , hard surface and maintain such roads from
low-water mark to high-water mark on the banks of such
streams , repealing all laws in conflict with this act , providing
penalties for its violation .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That where ferries are operated for fees on
streams in this Commonwealth , where roads and
public highways cross , other than those roads em-
braced in the State primary road system by being
actually surveyed and contracts let for the construc-
tion of same and State maintained highways , the
owners or lessees shall grade the banks of such
streams and hard surface same, at least sixteen feet
wide from the low water mark of such stream to
high water mark or the top of the bank of same in
such road and maintain said hard surface .
.
§ 2. Any owner or lessee of such ferry , whether
an individual , association , joint stock company , or
syndicate , failing to comply with the requirements

CHAPTER 441 .
AN ACT establishing as a part of the primary system of State
highways of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , a road from a
point upon U. S. Highway No. 60 , three miles southwest of
Henderson , to Mt. Vernon Ferry .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
1. That there is hereby established as a part of
the primary system of State Highways of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky , a road running from a point
upon U. S. Highway No. 60 , three miles south - west
of Henderson , Henderson county , thence via Geneva ,
Smith Mills and Alzey to Mount Vernon Ferry , con-
necting with Indiana State Highway No. 62 and other
concrete highways at Mt. Vernon , Ind ., same being
approximately 20.5 miles in length .
2. The construction , maintenance , supervision
and control of said road shall be subject to , and con-
trolled by all terms and conditions set out and pro-
vided for in chapter 17 of the Acts of the General
Assembly of 1920 , establishing and creating a pri-
mary system of State highways , the same as though
said road had been named in said original act as one
of the projects of the said primary system of State
highways .
§ 3. The highway commission is hereby directed
to assign to the above road an appropriate project
number .
Approved March 19 , 1928 .

entries.ocr_text:
842 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
has been the custom of the legislature to provide temporary
help for this department when needed , and whereas , the
largely increased business and changes at this time in the
various branches of government , in addition to the vast amount
of legislative printing now on hand , has greatly congested the
State's work in the Department of Public Printing , making it
a physical impossibility for the superintendent alone to take
care of it ; therefore ,
Be it resolved by the Senate , the House concur-
ring :
That the Superintendent of Public Printing , with
the approval of the Board of Printing Commission-
ers (composed of the Governor , Attorney General ,
Auditor, Treasurer and Secretary of State ) be
authorized to employ an assistant who , like himself ,
shall have a practical knowledge of printing , ruling ,
binding , estimating, and of office work generally , at
five dollars a day, and a combined bookkeeper - sten-
ographer at four dollars a day, their compensation
to be paid from legislative expenditures account
until the adjournment of the legislature .
The services of the Assistant to the Superinten-
dent of Public Printing shall be continued until the
end of the Fiscal year , June thirty, one thousand
nine hundred and twenty- eight , and the services of
the bookkeeper -stenographer shall be continued un-
til thirty days after the adjournment of this Legis-
lative session , and their compensation shall be at
the same rate per diem as provided herein for their
services rendered during the session , the same to
be paid from the account of public Printing and
Binding .
Approved February 24 , 1928 .

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 843
CHAPTER 584 .
CONCURRENT RESOLUTION of the Senate of Kentucky and
the House of Representatives of the General Assembly on the
payment of the per diem , mileage , stationery , etc. , due the late
Moorman B. Daniel, a member of the General Assembly.
Be it resolved by the Senate of Kentucky , and the
House of Representatives concurring herein :
THAT WHEREAS , Moorman B. Daniel , a mem-
ber of the General Assembly of the State of Ken-
tucky, died on the eleventh day of January , one
thousand nine hundred and twenty - eight , during the
session of the present General Assembly, and
WHEREAS , there was due him from the Common-
wealth his per diem , mileage , stationery and chair-
man of the Committee ,
AND WHEREAS , he left surviving him a sister,
Lucy Ellen Daniel , of Clinton , Hickman County ,
Kentucky , whose education ' the said Moorman B.
Daniel , was providing for , now
THEREFORE , Be it resolved by the Senate of Ken-
tucky, and the House of Representatives con-
curring herein , that up to the date of his death
there was due him from the Commonwealth the fol-
lowing sums : Per diem , Ninety Dollars . Mileage ,
One Hundred and One Dollars and Seventy Cents .
Stationery , Twenty Dollars . Chairman Committee ,
Five Dollars . Total , Two Hundred and Sixteen
Dollars and Seventy Cents . That said amount
should be payable to his estate and the balance for
his per diem had he served out his full term would
be Six Hundred and Thirty ( $ 630.00 ) Dollars ,
which sum shall be paid to his sister, Lucy Ellen
Daniel , of Clinton , Hickman County , Kentucky , and
the Clerk of the House is hereby directed to issue
his order on the Auditor for the said sums and pay-
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1928, p. 896

pose of which is to fully advise from day to day the officials
and public of the Commonwealth as to matters affecting them
in an intelligent and prompt manner ; therefore ,
Be it resolved by the Senate of Kentucky , the
House of Representatives concurring :
That the publishers of the Legislative Digest are
hereby directed to furnish daily a copy to each
member and the Clerks of the General Assembly ,
and to the heads of each State Department during
the time of this session . That the publishers are
allowed the sum of Ten Dollars for each subscrip-
tion for the Session and the Auditor of Public Ac-
counts is directed to draw his warrant upon the
Treasurer for said amount when such service has
been concluded . The total number shall not exceed
( 200 ) two hundred copies , which are to be paid for
under this resolution .
Approved February 24 , 1928 .

WHEREAS , it is the desire of the entire nation that suitable
recognition be given to the soldiers and sailors who gave
their lives in the defense of their country and the rights of
free peoples in the World War ; and
WHEREAS , the Commonwealth of Kentucky is a State rich in
tradition , being the birthplace of Abraham Lincoln , President
of the Union , in the most critical days of its history , and of
Jefferson Davis , President of the Confederacy ; and
WHEREAS , there is now a Federal Highway established in said
State beginning at Hodgensville , the birthplace of Abraham
Lincoln , where already a monument has been erected to his
memory , running thence to the Dixie Highway and passing
through the Mammoth Cave territory , which is now in process
of being created a National Park , thence through Russellville
and thence over the Jefferson Davis Highway westward
through Elkton to Fairview , Kentucky , where there is a State
Park already created and where a noble monument has been
erected to the memory of Jefferson Davis , thus connecting
the birthplaces of the two great leaders in the Civil War and
demonstrating the present unity of our nation , thence through
the city of Hopkinsville to the Government Hospital erected
for the Disabled World War Veterans at or near Dawson '
Springs, Kentucky ; and
WHEREAS , this route is rich in history in that it was the war
road established in the War of 1812 by ( General ) James E.
Jessup ; and
WHEREAS , the construction of a permanent road way of the
lasting endurance and character of the Appian Way , suitable
for military use in time of war and capable of sustaining the
movement of heavy artillery , would be a great benefit to the
entire country , and no more suitable or useful memorial could
be constructed by the United States in honor of its World
